bibliographic.title: The Ormulum, with the Notes and Glossary of Dr. R. M. White

entities.description: Robert Holt's revised two-volume edition. It retains White's text, notes, glossary, and long preface while correcting the text by fresh collation with the Bodleian manuscript and revising or extending the notes and glossary.
entities.label: White–Holt 1878 edition of The Ormulum
notes: Dekker identifies specific places where van Vliet's seventeenth-century alterations in Junius 1 entered White's and Holt's printed text. That indirect transmission through the altered principal witness is not evidence that either editor consulted Lambeth Palace Library MS 783.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1878, and U-M expressly marks its digitized item public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The 1878 edition's text, notes, glossary, and other published editorial matter

entities.description: The principal and autograph medieval witness. It preserves surviving portions of the intended first volume, including biblical narratives and homilies, with extensive insertions, deletions, and revisions in Orm's hand. A second hand added Latin pericopes and a few English additions. The manuscript is unfinished, extremely lacunose, and lacks a substantial ending.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Junius 1
notes: This is the only known medieval manuscript of the Ormulum and the upstream witness for White and Holt.

Jan van Vliet later annotated and altered this physical manuscript. Those interventions must be distinguished from Orm's autograph text and from van Vliet's separate notebook now cataloged as Lambeth MS 783.

physicalDescription.extent: [i] + ii + 117 + [i] leaves; manuscript proper fols. 3–118 and [119]
physicalDescription.format: Generally two columns, usually 50–70 lines per page
notes: Bound in modern pulp boards; the binding probably postdates significant losses suffered after Junius numbered the columns.

rights.basis: The late-twelfth-century work and manuscript are centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The medieval textual content embodied in the autograph manuscript
notes: This does not apply to modern Bodleian photography, catalog prose, interface assets, or provider terms.

entities.description: An early-modern miscellany containing 49 leaves of Ormulum excerpts and glossaries copied by Jan van Vliet, principally on ff. 51r–88r. The six relevant sections include miscellaneous excerpts, the Dedication, the verse preface, passages from the opening homily, and material from columns now lost. It is a partial seventeenth-century transcript of Junius 1, not an independent medieval witness.

holding.notes: The current public catalog search did not expose a stable item-specific record during this review; use the exact shelfmark in an enquiry.

entities.label: London, Lambeth Palace Library, MS 783 (van Vliet Ormulum excerpts)
notes: No relation from the 1852 or 1878 edition to this entity is asserted. Although selected van Vliet alterations made directly in Junius 1 entered White/Holt, the separate Lambeth transcript was brought to modern editorial attention only in the 1930s.
The 2023 edition uses only the Lambeth transcript's evidence for known missing portions and related readings; the principal surviving text remains the autograph Junius 1.

reviewNotes: The exact current complete manuscript object is Digital Bodleian 90a06f70-880a-4b5b-bd30-798710afff11 with 263 canvases. The superficially similar 8d37b5d4-27ec-4da0-8a9e-dc3cba62c212 record contains only two 35mm images.

Lambeth MS 783 is deliberately modeled as a partial seventeenth-century derivative transcript of Junius 1. No upstream relation from White 1852, Holt 1878, the U-M encoding, or the repository artifact to Lambeth 783 is asserted.

Some van Vliet readings nevertheless entered White/Holt indirectly because van Vliet altered the physical Junius 1 manuscript before White and Holt transcribed and collated it. That limited contamination is recorded in notes and evidence rather than turning Lambeth 783 into an editorial source.

The item-specific 2006 XML production statement says uncorrected OCR; the generic U-M collection narrative says the broader batch was re-keyed and proofread. The item-specific evidence governs this record while the discrepancy remains open.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item AFW5744.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR/TEI Level 1 encoding linked to 1,104 page images of the two-volume 1878 Clarendon Press edition. Robert Holt's 1878 edition revised Robert Meadows White's 1852 edition and newly collated its text against the principal and autograph witness, Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Junius 1. The complete manuscript is publicly digitized by the Bodleian. Jan van Vliet's partial seventeenth-century transcript in Lambeth Palace Library MS 783 is not an independent medieval witness and was not a source for White/Holt; it is recorded because it preserves passages copied before leaves disappeared and is used selectively in the 2023 Johannesson–Cooper comparison edition.

catalogSummary.author: Orm
catalogSummary.region: East Midlands; Bourne Abbey in south Lincolnshire has been proposed
catalogSummary.title: The Ormulum

catalogingSubject.label: The Ormulum
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The late-twelfth-century Middle English work attributed to Orm: its dedication, list of planned Gospel texts, verse preface, biblical narratives, and homiletic exposition. This subject is not the autograph manuscript, either nineteenth-century edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML file.

contentParts.label: Dedication to Walter
contentParts.summary: Orm addresses his brother Walter, explains the commission and intended use of the work, describes his method of translating and explaining Gospel readings, and directs future copyists to preserve his spelling and doubled letters.

contentParts.label: List of 242 planned texts
notes: The list is incomplete at its end in the autograph and does not prove that all 242 planned treatments were composed.
contentParts.summary: A Latin list of Gospel and Acts pericopes planned for treatment in the work; its extent documents the intended program rather than the number of units now surviving.

contentParts.label: Verse preface
contentParts.summary: The 106-line preface names the book Orrmulum because Orrm made it and introduces its Gospel-centered spiritual program.

contentParts.label: Surviving biblical narratives and homilies
notes: The source XML labels this entire DIV1 as introduction, but its 22 child divisions contain the surviving narrative and homiletic body; the label is not treated as a work-level genre or structure claim.
contentParts.summary: The surviving verse body begins with an unheaded introduction and continues through 21 numbered units represented by Gospel lemmata numbered I, III, VII, XI, XIV–XVI, XVIII–XXVIII, XXX–XXXII. It ends in fragments after the heading for John XXXII.

assertion.notes: The MED bibliography redated its stencil to c.1175 in 2017 after consultation with the OED; the wider normalized range preserves Oxford's less compressed formulation.

dateStatements.display: c. 1175; Oxford describes the work as written in the 1160s and 1170s

notes: This is the Bodleian's date for the autograph manuscript. Because author and principal scribe are the same person, composition and first attestation overlap, but they remain distinct descriptive claims.

extent.description: The local U-M-derived XML encodes 20,531 L elements in 159 LG elements: 342 lines in the dedication, 106 in the verse preface, and 20,083 in the surviving introduction/narrative/homily body. It separately lists 242 planned pericopes and divides the surviving body into 22 XML items (one unheaded introduction plus 21 numbered units). These are metrics of this representation of a lacunose, unfinished work, not the extent of Orm's original plan.

formStatements.description: Metrical biblical narratives and homilies in unrhymed fifteen-syllable long lines, conventionally printed as paired half-lines. The ancillary Latin pericope list and headings do not change the primary literary form.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML header's single eng language declaration omits both the Middle English code enm and the embedded Latin established by the text and Bodleian metadata.

scope.description: The list of 242 planned pericopes and the Latin Gospel lemmata introducing surviving homiletic units

notes: The U-M source description encodes White and Holt in AUTHOR elements because it describes the 1878 book; the title pages and preface establish them as editors, while the medieval preface and Bodleian catalog establish Orm as author.
The XML's root language declaration eng is not reused mechanically: the work is Middle English (enm) with embedded Latin.
The XML's broad DIV1 TYPE='introduction' label covers the surviving narrative and homiletic body and is treated as an encoding label, not a reliable work-level structural title.

Access and rights for the medieval text, nineteenth-century editions, modern U-M encoding, Bodleian manuscript images, Lambeth transcript, and 2023 edition are kept separate in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review Malcolm Parkes's argument and subsequent paleographical, dialectal, and institutional scholarship.
Preserve competing place proposals as separate assertions rather than replacing the current tentative statement.
openQuestions.question: Can Orm's institutional setting and place of composition be established more securely than the proposed Bourne Abbey localization?

next_steps: Consult the 2023 edition's introduction and projected commentary volume for its reconstruction of the work's compositional stages.
Keep planned extent, once-written-but-lost material, and currently extant text as separate quantities.
openQuestions.question: How much of the 242-item plan did Orm compose, and how much of the mismatch with the surviving text results from medieval incompletion versus later loss?

notes: A linguistic region should not be treated automatically as the exact place of composition.

assertion.notes: The Bodleian catalog and MED bibliography attribute this proposal to Malcolm Parkes and mark it as uncertain.

notes: The work explicitly names Orrm as its maker; this responsibility must not be reassigned to White or Holt because the U-M bibliographic header lists them in AUTHOR elements.

scope.description: White's 1852 edition, retained as the textual, annotational, and lexicographical basis of Holt's 1878 source edition
The revised 1878 edition used by the U-M encoding
Correction of White's text by renewed collation against Bodleian MS Junius 1

subjects.label: History of English language, spelling, and sound

summaries.value: A late-twelfth-century Middle English verse homiliary by the Augustinian canon Orm. At his brother Walter's request, Orm renders Gospel readings into English and follows each narrative with explanation intended to support preaching and Christian instruction. The work is especially important for its highly systematic spelling and evidence for Early Middle English pronunciation.

The surviving autograph is both unfinished and extremely lacunose. Its table plans at least 242 biblical narratives followed by homilies; the CME encoding represents an introductory division and 21 numbered surviving units through number 32, with large internal losses and a fragmentary ending. Van Vliet's seventeenth-century partial transcript preserves passages from leaves later lost, but it is not a coequal medieval witness.

assertion.notes: The verse preface says that the book is named Orrmulum because Orrm made it; modern editions and catalogs commonly regularize the title to Ormulum.

work.author: Orm

publication.embeddedTitle: The Ormulum / with the notes and glossary of R.M. White; edited by Robert Holt.

work.regionDisplay: East Midlands; Bourne Abbey in south Lincolnshire has been proposed

At his brother Walter's request, Orm renders Gospel readings into English and follows each narrative with explanation intended to support preaching and Christian instruction.
